cards.front: Name the 5 RSTP port roles and how they differ from classic STP.
cards.back: Root, Designated (same as 802.1D), plus: Alternate (backup path to root if primary fails), Backup (backup designated on a shared segment — rare), Disabled. RSTP merges Blocking + Listening into a single 'Discarding' state, drastically speeding convergence.

cards.front: Name the 3 RSTP port states (instead of classic STP's 5).
cards.back: Discarding (replaces Disabled, Blocking, Listening), Learning, Forwarding. Sub-second convergence vs 802.1D's 30-50s.

cards.front: What is an RSTP edge port and what feature on Cisco accomplishes the same thing?
cards.back: Edge port = port connected to an end device (PC/server), not another switch. Skips the transition through Discarding/Learning and goes straight to Forwarding. Cisco implements this as `spanning-tree portfast` (per-port) or `spanning-tree portfast default` (global, on all access ports).

cards.front: What does BPDU Guard do, and where is it used?
cards.back: If a port configured with `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` receives a BPDU (meaning someone plugged a switch into an end-user port), the port goes err-disabled. Used on edge/PortFast ports to prevent rogue switches from joining the topology.

cards.front: What does Root Guard do, and how does it differ from BPDU Guard?
cards.back: Root Guard prevents a port from accepting a 'superior' BPDU that would make a downstream switch the new root. If a superior BPDU is received, the port goes 'root-inconsistent' (effectively blocking) until BPDUs stop. Used on DESIGNATED ports facing customer/access switches. BPDU Guard, by contrast, err-disables on ANY BPDU.

cards.front: Configure an LACP EtherChannel using ports Gi0/1-2.
cards.back:
```
interface range gi0/1 - 2
 channel-protocol lacp
 channel-group 1 mode active
```
Other mode is `passive`. For PAgP use modes `desirable`/`auto`. For static (no negotiation): `channel-group 1 mode on`. Both ends must use compatible modes.

cards.front: EtherChannel mode matrix: which combinations form a bundle?
cards.back: LACP: active+active ✓, active+passive ✓, passive+passive ✗. PAgP: desirable+desirable ✓, desirable+auto ✓, auto+auto ✗. Static: on+on ✓ (no negotiation; misconfig risk). Mixing protocols (LACP+PAgP) NEVER works.

cards.front: What configuration must match across member ports of an EtherChannel?
cards.back: Speed, duplex, allowed VLANs, native VLAN, switchport mode (access/trunk), encapsulation (dot1q), STP port cost/priority. Mismatches cause the bundle to suspend the offending port. Bundle a maximum of 8 active links per EtherChannel.

cards.front: How do you verify EtherChannel status, and what flags should you see for healthy LACP?
cards.back: `show etherchannel summary` — flag column should read `(SU)` for the channel (S=Layer 2, U=in use) and each member port should show `P` (bundled). Other flags: `s` (suspended), `H` (hot-standby in LACP), `I` (individual / not bundled — bad sign).

cards.front: What is Loop Guard and when do you use it?
cards.back: When a port stops receiving BPDUs (e.g., unidirectional fiber failure), it could transition to forwarding and create a loop. Loop Guard puts the port into 'loop-inconsistent' state instead of letting it transition. Used on root and alternate ports of point-to-point links. Configured per port: `spanning-tree guard loop` or globally with `spanning-tree loopguard default`.

cards.front: What does BPDU Filter do, and how does it differ in interface vs global config?
cards.back: BPDU Filter prevents a port from sending and processing BPDUs. Interface-level (`spanning-tree bpdufilter enable`): port silently ignores received BPDUs and sends none — DANGEROUS, can cause loops if mis-applied. Global default (`spanning-tree portfast bpdufilter default`): applied only to PortFast ports; if such a port RECEIVES a BPDU, both PortFast and BPDU Filter are disabled and the port becomes a normal STP port (safer).

cards.front: What is ErrDisable Recovery and how do you configure it?
cards.back: Cisco feature that automatically re-enables ports that were placed in err-disabled state (e.g., by BPDU Guard, port-security violation). View status: `show errdisable recovery`. Default interval = 300 s. Configure: `errdisable recovery interval <sec>`, and per cause `errdisable recovery cause bpduguard` (or psecure-violation, etc.). Manual recovery is `shutdown` then `no shutdown`.

cards.front: RSTP Alternate vs Backup port — how are they distinguished?
cards.back: Both are 'discarding' (blocking) backups. Alternate port: receives a SUPERIOR BPDU from a different switch — backup for the root port (alt path to the root). Backup port: receives a superior BPDU from the SAME switch (only possible on a shared/hub segment) — backup for a designated port. Alternates are common; backups are rare on full-duplex links.

cards.front: RSTP / Multiple STP IEEE numbers, and Cisco's flavors?
cards.back: RSTP = IEEE 802.1w. MST/MSTP = IEEE 802.1s. Cisco PVST+ = Cisco's per-VLAN extension of 802.1D. Cisco Rapid PVST+ = per-VLAN 802.1w. To enable: `spanning-tree mode rapid-pvst` (or `pvst`, or `mst`).

cards.front: RSTP link types (edge / point-to-point / shared) — what are they and when?
cards.back: Edge: connects to an end host (PC), skips discarding/learning — set with `spanning-tree portfast`. Point-to-point: full-duplex switch-to-switch link, fast convergence via proposal/agreement (`spanning-tree link-type point-to-point`, default for full-duplex). Shared: half-duplex (legacy hub) — slow, classic STP-like behavior (`spanning-tree link-type shared`, default for half-duplex).

cards.front: Memorize RSTP (long) port costs: 10M, 100M, 1G, 10G, 100G, 1T.
cards.back: 10 Mbps = 2,000,000. 100 Mbps = 200,000. 1 Gbps = 20,000. 10 Gbps = 2,000. 100 Gbps = 200. 1 Tbps = 20. (10 Tbps = 2.) RSTP uses long path-cost method by default; values fall by 10× per speed jump.

cards.front: Loop Guard vs Root Guard interaction on the same port?
cards.back: They are mutually exclusive — a port can have one, not both. Configuring `spanning-tree guard root` overrides any prior `spanning-tree guard loop`. To remove either, use `spanning-tree guard none`. Globally: `spanning-tree loopguard default` enables Loop Guard on all ports, but a per-interface guard root command takes precedence on that port.

cards.front: EtherChannel — what does LAG / PAgP / LACP stand for, and what IEEE standard is LACP?
cards.back: LAG = Link Aggregation Group (industry term for EtherChannel). PAgP = Port Aggregation Protocol (Cisco proprietary, modes: desirable/auto). LACP = Link Aggregation Control Protocol = IEEE 802.3ad (industry standard, modes: active/passive). LACP supports up to 8 active + 8 standby links per bundle.

cards.front: EtherChannel — must channel-group numbers match between switches? Must port count match?
cards.back: Channel-group numbers do NOT need to match between the two switches (locally significant). Member-port count and configuration (speed, duplex, switchport mode, allowed VLANs, native VLAN, dot1q encap, STP cost) MUST match between the two switches and across local member ports.

cards.front: EtherChannel load-balancing methods on Cisco?
cards.back: src-mac, dst-mac, src-dst-mac, src-ip, dst-ip, src-dst-ip (and on newer hardware src/dst port). Default varies by platform (often src-mac on L2 switches). Configured globally: `port-channel load-balance <method>`. View: `show etherchannel load-balance`.

cards.front: Configure a Layer-3 EtherChannel?
cards.back: Configure the port-channel as a routed interface — first `no switchport` on each member, then `channel-group N mode <mode>`. Then on the port-channel interface itself: `no switchport` and `ip address ...`. Used between L3 switches/routers without a VLAN.
